skill.name: chatgpt-app-builder
skill.description: Deprecated ChatGPT app development guide for building MCP servers and interactive widgets with mcp-use and the OpenAI Apps SDK. It directs users to mcp-app-builder for current usage and keeps references for discovery, setup, widget design, state, CSP, and UI patterns.
skill.summary: Build ChatGPT MCP apps with widget planning, setup, state, UI, and Apps SDK reference guidance.
skill.author: mcp-use

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-severity patterns, but review found they are mostly Markdown examples, API names, code fences, and license text rather than executable malicious code. The confirmed risk is legitimate developer guidance that may run npm/npx commands, start tunnels, use external URLs, and read environment-driven configuration. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Developer Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents and users to install skills, scaffold projects, install packages, start tunnels, and deploy apps. This is expected for an app-builder skill, but commands can change the filesystem, install dependencies, and expose a local service if executed without review.
medium_findings.recommendation: Publish with a warning that users should review commands, dependencies, tunnel exposure, and deployment targets before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and semantically relevant to the skill. They are legitimate development commands, not evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External Network and Tunnel Configuration
medium_findings.description: The references guide users to local MCP endpoints, tunnels, external checkout links, and CSP allowlists for third-party domains. This is legitimate for ChatGPT apps, but it increases review requirements for network exposure and data sharing.
medium_findings.recommendation: Require users to approve external domains, tunnels, and checkout destinations. Prefer exact HTTPS domains and avoid broad CSP allowlists.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is directly documented and expected for app integration. The reviewed files do not show credential exfiltration or hidden destinations.

low_findings.title: Environment Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The skill shows environment-based URL configuration for MCP base URLs and optional CSP domains. This is common configuration guidance, but users should avoid placing secrets in generated client-visible metadata or logs.
low_findings.recommendation: Document that environment variables should contain configuration, not secrets exposed to widgets or generated output.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples access environment-driven configuration, but only for URLs and CSP domains. No evidence found that secrets are read or transmitted.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static findings labeled weak cryptography occur in documentation, evaluation text, and license prose. The reviewed files contain no executable cryptographic algorithm selection or hashing implementation.
low_findings.recommendation: Dismiss these blocker alerts. Do not block publication based on weak-crypto pattern matches in prose.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are prose or eval expected behavior, not executable crypto code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar usage in runtime code.

low_findings.title: Backtick Execution Alerts Are Markdown and JavaScript Examples
low_findings.description: Many Ruby backtick alerts are Markdown fences, inline code, or JavaScript template strings inside documentation. They are not Ruby shell execution and are not packaged runtime code.
low_findings.recommendation: Dismiss Ruby backtick findings where the syntax is Markdown fencing or TypeScript template interpolation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation snippets and fenced examples. No Ruby source file or shell backtick execution was found.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Responsive UI Context
low_findings.description: The system-reconnaissance findings correspond to widget host context such as device type, safe area, locale, and display mode. This supports responsive UI behavior and is documented as part of the widget API.
low_findings.recommendation: Allow host context usage for layout and localization. Review generated apps if they persist or transmit this data externally.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is limited to documented widget environment fields. No evidence found of OS probing, file enumeration, or unauthorized host reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Critical Combination Dismissed After Context Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The critical heuristic combined command examples, network examples, and environment configuration from documentation. Review found no obfuscation, no credential access beyond URL configuration examples, and no exfiltration logic.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Do not block on the combined heuristic. Keep medium publication warning for command and network exposure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky categories are present, but they appear in explanatory examples for building apps. No malicious intent or hidden runtime behavior was found.

content.value_statement: Building ChatGPT apps with widgets requires careful tool design, state handling, and host metadata. This skill guides developers through discovery, setup, React widgets, MCP tools, CSP, and UI patterns.
actual_capabilities: Guides product discovery before building a ChatGPT app.
Maps user flows to MCP tools and interactive widgets.
Shows how to scaffold and run an mcp-use app project.
Explains React widget metadata, props, state, and tool calls.
Documents CSP, Apps SDK metadata, display modes, themes, and layout constraints.
Provides manual evaluation prompts for checking skill behavior.
limitations: The skill is deprecated and recommends mcp-app-builder for current projects.
It provides guidance and examples, not a complete production application.
Generated apps still require security review, authentication design, and deployment checks.
Some examples use placeholder APIs and local development URLs.

use_cases.title: Plan a focused ChatGPT app
use_cases.description: Define the user problem, core actions, LLM value, and product context before implementation.

use_cases.title: Implement MCP tools and widgets
use_cases.description: Create React widgets with metadata, props, loading states, persistent state, and tool calls.

use_cases.title: Prepare an Apps SDK integration
use_cases.description: Configure local development, connector testing, CSP domains, and ChatGPT-specific metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Start app discovery
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a ChatGPT app for [business or product]. Ask discovery questions one phase at a time.

prompt_templates.title: Choose tools and widgets
prompt_templates.prompt: Given this app spec, decide which flows need widgets and which should stay as MCP tools: [spec].

prompt_templates.title: Design widget behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Design the widget metadata, props, persistent state, loading behavior, and tool calls for this flow: [flow].

prompt_templates.title: Review integration readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my ChatGPT app plan for CSP domains, Apps SDK metadata, display modes, auth needs, and deployment risks: [plan].

output_examples.input: I want to build a ChatGPT app for a restaurant reservation service.
output: A discovery plan with the target user, pain point, core reservation actions, API needs, and authentication questions.
A recommendation to use a search results widget and a reservation tool for backend booking.

output_examples.input: How should my flight booking app use widgets and tools?
output: A flow map that keeps search, comparison, and seat selection in one widget.
A separate checkout tool for the backend payment action.

output_examples.input: My widget needs state, dark mode, and an external checkout link.
output: Guidance to use persistent widget state for selected items, theme-aware styling, and host-approved external navigation.
A reminder to declare required external domains in CSP metadata.

best_practices: Start with discovery and keep the app focused on one to three core actions.
Return needed widget data upfront and avoid duplicate tool calls for the same data.
Declare exact CSP domains and test widgets in both light and dark themes.
anti_patterns: Porting a full web application into ChatGPT instead of building focused actions.
Using backend tools for widget-only state such as selections, filters, or hover behavior.
Adding broad CSP domains, unsafe script directives, or undeclared external resources.

faq.question: Is this skill current?
faq.answer: No. It is deprecated and recommends using mcp-app-builder for current ChatGPT app work.

faq.question: Does it create a complete app automatically?
faq.answer: No. It guides planning and implementation. Developers still build, test, secure, and deploy the app.

faq.question: What frameworks does it cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on mcp-use, React widgets, MCP servers, and OpenAI Apps SDK metadata.

faq.question: Can widgets call backend tools?
faq.answer: Yes. The references explain widget tool calls and when backend tools should handle real actions.

faq.question: Does it cover ChatGPT connector setup?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes local development, tunnel usage, ChatGPT app settings, and connector testing guidance.

faq.question: What security work remains for generated apps?
faq.answer: Review authentication, authorization, CSP domains, external APIs, secrets, deployment settings, and user data handling.
